feedback.text: The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.

sections.title: Unfair commercial practices. Platform responsibilities and accountability
path: Unfair commercial practices. Platform responsibilities and accountability

paragraphs.text: M
FUNDACIÓN MALDITA.ES PERIODISMO PARA QUE NO TE LA CUELEN

Maldita.es is a non-profit foundation based in Spain that builds public trust and protects information integrity through journalism, education, technology, research and policy action. Our work is underscored by specialised teams, cutting edge technological tools, and an extensive community of citizens who collaborate with us in the battle against disinformation.

Our mission is to provide all actors affected—from legislators and digital platforms to journalists, citizens & educators—with tools, capacities, and evidence-based research so that they can make informed decisions, and together we can foster a more resilient, accessible, and trustworthy media & information ecosystem.
Contact point: policy@maldita.es

The Digital Fairness Act (DFA) represents a crucial opportunity to address the structural features of digital environments that facilitate the spread of disinformation and undermine informed public debate. While existing frameworks such as the Digital Services Act (DSA) and the AI Act already impose certain obligations on platforms, the DFA can fill critical gaps by targeting manipulative and unfair design practices that distort users' choices and amplify false or harmful content.

By tackling dark patterns, addictive design features, unfair personalisation, covert influencer marketing, and deceptive pricing, the DFA can strengthen the transparency, accountability, and integrity of the online information ecosystem. Effective enforcement of these measures would not only enhance consumer protection but also limit the systemic incentives that reward engagement over accuracy, thereby reducing citizens' exposure to manipulation and disinformation.

A strong and coherent Digital Fairness Act can therefore serve as a cornerstone of the EU's broader strategy to ensure that digital markets remain both competitive and trustworthy, supporting citizens' capacity to make informed, independent decisions in the digital sphere.

The Digital Services Act (DSA) has prohibited dark patterns since February 2024, but we observe limited change in practice. We urge stronger supervision and enforcement so that these prohibitions are effectively implemented and penalties are applied where needed. Dark patterns make it harder for consumers to distinguish advertising from information and facilitate exposure to mis- and disinformation. We recommend systematic enforcement and clear user interface standards that prevent nudging toward engagement at the expense of accuracy.
M
FUNDACIÓN MALDITA.ES PERIODISMO PARA QUE NO TE LA CUELEN

Academic evidence confirms that manipulative interface designs facilitate the exposure and spread of disinformation. This study shows how engagement-driven interface features on Facebook promote toxic behaviour and disinformation. Similarly, this report highlights that dark patterns make it easy to share false or harmful content while hiding options for critical verification, thereby eroding trust in news and democratic discourse.
To make these rules work in practice, we believe platforms should follow some basic design principles:

They should make sure their interfaces are fair and don't trick people. For example, by avoiding countdown timers, pre-ticked boxes or layouts that push users to accept less privacy or lower-quality information.
Transparency labels for ads, political ads and AI-generated or synthetic content should be easy to see and understand, as visible as the content itself.
Before launching big design changes that affect how people find or share information, platforms should test them with independent experts and civil society groups.

As this paper on Computer Law & Security Review argues, digital vulnerability to manipulative design is multidimensional and cannot be reduced to age or socioeconomic status. EU technology regulatory frameworks should embed a "fair-by-design" approach to increase users' resilience to dark patterns. Recent work expands this view, identifying 68 unique dark pattern types, of which more than half escape current detection tools. This supports the need for systematic EU-level auditing obligations, and the Digital Fairness Act should embrace that.

Platforms often prioritise polarising content, and this addictive design can amplify attention capture and contribute to informational radicalisation. We support targeted rules against engagement-maximising defaults (infinite scroll, autoplay, streak penalties) and clear opt-outs.

Recent academic evidence establishes a causal link between engagement-driven design and polarisation. Field experiments demonstrate that social media usage, particularly algorithmic feeds, increases affective polarisation. A 2024 audit of TikTok's algorithm found that platform recommendation systems actively promote self-radicalisation pathways, while a 2022 systematic review on YouTube concluded that recommender systems facilitate access to extremist and conspiratorial content.

At the same time, research highlights that users' vulnerability to manipulative design and deceptive persuasion depends on intersecting social and educational inequalities. The absence of clear disclosure obligations therefore disproportionately harms less digitally literate audiences, who may be less able to recognise sponsored or manipulative content.
M
FUNDACIÓN MALDITA.ES PERIODISMO PARA QUE NO TE LA CUELEN
To reduce the impact of addictive design features, especially on young people, platforms should make some simple but effective changes.

For minors, features like endless scrolling and autoplay should be turned off by default. There should also be small pauses or reminders that make users think twice before sharing large amounts of content in a short time.

Platforms should offer clear “wellbeing” tools. For example, dashboards that help people see and manage how much time they spend online (and these tools should be developed with input from independent experts).
When platforms identify serious risks such as polarisation or disinformation, they should be required to take concrete action and publicly report what they have done to reduce those risks.

In fact, a recent ruling in the Netherlands compels Meta Platforms to provide users a direct, easily-accessible option to switch to a non-algorithmic, chronological feed, and to ensure this preference persists across sessions, without automatically reverting to an algorithmic feed when the app is reopened. This landmark decision underscores that addictive or attention-maximizing design elements—especially hidden defaults or automatic reversion to algorithmic prioritization—can be legally challenged as “dark patterns” that impair user autonomy.

Hidden advertising and harmful claims by influencers remain a persistent problem. A UN survey cited in the draft highlights that 62% of influencers do not verify information and 42% rely on likes or shares as credibility signals. This underscores the urgent need for EU-level harmonisation of disclosure rules, traceability of paid partnerships, and effective enforcement mechanisms.

Empirical evidence confirms that influencer marketing continues to be a major vector for disinformation and covert advertising. Recent large-scale analysis of over 100 million posts on Twitter found that 96% of sponsored content lacked disclosure. Similarly, documented influencer campaigns, such as Fazze's anti-vaccine operation, and studies on the promotion of unproven health tests illustrate the persistence of covert commercial disinformation. These findings are consistent with a large-scale audit of online content showing that only about 10% of advertising material on YouTube and Pinterest was properly disclosed to users. This lack of transparency facilitates the spread of deceptive endorsements and manipulative narratives disguised as authentic content.

Broader analyses, such as Safeguarding Democracy Against Disinformation, further show that influencers are often integrated into networks of “amplifiers” that artificially boost disinformation campaigns, including digital astroturfing and undisclosed political advertising. These dynamics blur the boundaries between opinion, sponsorship, and factual information, ultimately eroding citizens’ trust in digital media.
In light of this evidence, several clear rules should apply across all platforms. All paid or sponsored content should use a single, standard label, such as “Paid partnership” or “Ad”,
M
FUNDACIÓN MALDITA.ES PERIODISMO PARA QUE NO TE LA CUELEN

placed prominently so that viewers cannot miss it. There should also be quick and simple procedures for users to request a correction when they are harmed by false or misleading influencer content, and platforms should ensure that such corrections reach audiences as widely as the original post. Finally, platforms should clearly indicate who is paying for any influencer content, enabling users to identify the real source behind each promotion.

Evidence from Spain offers a practical reference for such mechanisms. The Spanish *right of rectification*, updated in March 2025, allows individuals to demand that a correction be disseminated within three days under the same conditions as the original false information, unless the publisher can justify its accuracy; otherwise, legal action is possible. We suggest exploring an EU-level model that adapts these safeguards for influencer content and platform environments, ensuring rapid and transparent remedies against misleading digital communication.

Personalised systems can prioritise polarising content and political disinformation. Empirical *studies* consistently find that algorithmic personalisation amplifies polarising and conspiratorial content. Further *evidence* confirms that manipulative user interfaces facilitate disinformation by overwhelming credible news signals. There is also evidence of the use of proxies to infer GDPR specially protected data like ideology to personalize content. These findings confirm the systemic risks identified under the DSA and justify stronger transparency and opt-out requirements for personalised recommender systems.
To make personalisation fairer and more transparent, platforms should meet some basic standards.

Not to personalize services on the basis of data that can be used as a proxy for protected categories within GDPR, such as ideology, which is often inferred from other categories of data with lesser legal protections

They should not use people's vulnerabilities or sensitive traits, such as emotions, financial situation or beliefs, to target content or ads, and any form of high-impact personalisation should only happen if users clearly agree to it.
People should always be able to understand why they are seeing a certain post, video or price, and have easy options to switch to a simple chronological or non-personalised feed.

Researchers and fact-checkers should have independent access to the data they need to study how personalisation affects information quality, building on the transparency tools already created by the Digital Services Act.

paragraphs.text: In May 2025, *Maldita.es* detected a network of fraudulent Facebook pages that impersonated public transport services. We flagged *58 fraudulent Facebook posts to Meta* using DSA mechanisms; a week later, 93% were still active, despite violating Meta's own

paragraphs.text: rules. Reporting "misleading or scam" was one-click easy, but reporting "unlawful" content was slow and often unresolved. This underscores the need for transparent, timely, and effective notice-and-action and appeals, and accountability for VLOPs in fraud, false advertising and scams.

section_path: Unfair commercial practices. Platform responsibilities and accountability
paragraphs.text: To make these systems actually work for citizens and watchdogs, we suggest three simple improvements:

section_path: Unfair commercial practices. Platform responsibilities and accountability
paragraphs.text: Faster and clearer responses. Platforms should be required to answer trusted reporters and fact-checkers within a fixed deadline, and make the results of those reports easy to verify.

section_path: Unfair commercial practices. Platform responsibilities and accountability
paragraphs.text: Real consequences for repeat offenders. Accounts that repeatedly post scams or deceptive ads should be suspended and verified before being allowed to advertise again.

section_path: Unfair commercial practices. Platform responsibilities and accountability
paragraphs.text: Unified and transparent reporting. All illegal or harmful content should be reportable through one single, simple form that lets users track what happens to their report from start to finish.

section_path: Unfair commercial practices. Platform responsibilities and accountability
paragraphs.text: These practical steps would make platform accountability real, ensuring that the rules meant to protect people from scams and false advertising are not just written on paper, but enforced effectively and transparently.

section_path: Unfair commercial practices. Platform responsibilities and accountability
paragraphs.text: All these are major problems that may be exploiting people's vulnerabilities and need to be addressed. The Digital Fairness Act can play a decisive role in it. It should avoid duplication by focusing on specific gaps and ensuring that digital design and commercial practices respect users' autonomy and the integrity of information, but also offer specific solutions to current problems in both the recitals and the articles, going beyond general obligations related to risk. Its success will depend on effective enforcement, meaningful transparency, and independent oversight.

section_path: Unfair commercial practices. Platform responsibilities and accountability
paragraphs.text: By embedding fairness and accountability into the architecture of digital services, the DFA can help rebuild public trust and strengthen Europe's resilience against deceptive and manipulative online environments.
